message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The objective of this paper is to examine the influence that various contextual variables have upon the number of deaths due to covid-19, across the world.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting Level</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study utilizes data for 125 countries for contextual variables from 1st January 2020 until the 15th June 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study considers deaths from covid-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interventions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>DELETED</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary and secondary outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The contextual variables considered in this study are stringency index, stringency variability, lockdown date, population density, level of airline passengers and country health security index.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>It is shown there is a very strong association between the level of airline passengers and covid-19 deaths. The results from regression analysis conducted in this study show significant positive relationships at the 5% level of statistical significance between Deaths from covid-19 and airline passenger levels and stringency variability; significant negative relationships are revealed for stringency index and lockdown date supporting the notion that lock down and social distancing measures mattered and were effective. The Global health security index and population density did not significantly affect deaths.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study highlights the strong link between a country’s airline passengers and covid-19 deaths and found that the lockdown date and stringency measures had a significant effect upon deaths. The implications of the research is that lockdown and stringency measures implemented by governments around the world worked and mattered. Further, the fact that global health security did not affect deaths may indicate better preparedness required to confront future pandemics.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Trial Registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>DELETED</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="order">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>FUNDING: This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial or not-for-profit sectors.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Article Summary: Strengths and Limitations of Study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>It is discovered in this paper, for a sample of 125 countries that lockdown and social distancing measures had a very significant positive effect upon reducing covid-19 deaths across the world. Countries deaths were very significantly positively related to the level of annual airline passengers. A combination of 18 countries with a share of 84% of global annual air passengers accounted for 80% of total deaths recorded from covid-19, worldwide.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The quality of a country’s health system as measured by a new measure GHI Global Health Security Index did not significantly reduce the number of covid-19 deaths, supporting the fact that both developed and developing countries were lacking in essential equipment, as well as track and trace mechanisms.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        The strengths of the study are that it is very timely and judgement needs to be made at the national and international level whether the lockdown was effective given the likelihood of a 2
                        <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                        wave to the pandemic. Countries need to better prepare themselves for future pandemics in terms of rapid data sharing and analysis to ensure that outbreaks are contained more effectively and efficiently through stringent lockdown and social distancing measures.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>A limitation of the study is the quality of data relating to the deaths actually caused by the covid-19 virus. Despite this the methodology and results of the paper are very sound and robust.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Global Determinants of Covid-19 Deaths: Lockdown Dates and Social Distancing Measures Mattered

reference.unstructured: World Bank Development Indicators (2019). Available from: [https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-indicators]
World Air Transport Statistics (2019). Available from: [https://www.iata.org/contentassets/a686ff624550453e8bf0c9b3f7f0ab26/wats-2019-mediakit.pdf]

Blavatnik School of Government, Oxford University. (2020). Coronavirus Government Response Tracker. Available from: [https://www.bsg.ox.ac.uk/research/research-projects/coronavirus-government-response-tracker].
Kaggle (2020). Lockdown Dates by Country. Available from: [https://www.kaggle.com/jcyzag/covid19-lockdown-dates-by-country]
Global Health Security Index (2019). Index data Available from: [https://www.ghsindex.org/]
Worldometers (2020). Reported Cases and Deaths by Country, Territory, or Conveyance. [ https://www.worldometers.info/coronavirus]
